coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots for long-term near-infrared afterglow imaging and photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zixuan
Pei, Qing
Zheng, Yonghui
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Imaging-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT) has the features of precise location and non-invasiveness. However, most of the photosensitizers (PSs) for PDT are poor fluorescent emitters, and the inadequate fluorescence is usually interfered by tissue auto-fluorescence. The afterglow phosphorescence could ideally address the issues of fluorescence imaging. Therefore, it is of double benefit to design phosphorescentCDs for enhanced near-infrared (NIR) phototheranostics. Hereon, we synthesized CDs with excellent biosafety, outstanding stability, good water dispersibility and effective photodynamic activity. CDs could emit NIR luminescence with long lifetime over 2 h, which afforded large tissue penetration (10 mm) and high SNR (98.4) for deep-tissue and long-term in vivo persistent luminescent imaging. Moreover, abundant reactive oxygen species generated by CDs enabled NIR afterglow imaging-guided PDT to effectively inhibit tumor growth. This work demonstrates the enormous potential of CDs as multifunctional nanoplatforms for biomedical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
near-infrared (NIR)
Afterglow imaging
photodynamic therapy (PDT)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo anti-tumor therapy and biosafety

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs aqueous solution (5 μg mL−1). (c) Bright-field fluorescence (λex = 400 nm, λem = 680 nm) and afterglow (after irradiation with 685 nm of laser, 0.8 W cm−2, 1 min) images of CDs (500 μg mL−1). (d) Full Scan XPS spectrum of CDs. High-resolution (e) C 1 s, (f) N 1 s XPS spectra of CDs.

Afterglow images of CDs (1 mg mL−1) (a) under the irradiation of laser (0.8 W cm−2) for different time points and (b) irradiated by different power density of laser for 3 min. (c) Afterglow images of CDs (0.1, 0.5 and 1.0 mg mL−1) irradiated by laser (0.8 W cm−2) for 3 min. The fluorescence spectra of DCFH-DA (d) with or (e) without CDs (50 μg mL−1) after irradiation with a 685 nm laser (0.1 W cm−2) from 0 to 240 s. (f) The decay rate of DCFH-DA at 530 nm under irradiation (685 nm laser, 0.1 W cm−2). (g) CLSM images of CT26 cells with photo-induced intracellular ROS generation.

(a) CLSM images of CT26 cells after incubation with CDs (60 μg mL−1). (b) Afterglow images of CT26 treated with CDs (1 mg mL−1) for 4 h. (c) MTT assay of CT26 cells treated with different concentrations of CDs under dark or laser irradiation (685 nm, 0.6 W cm−2). (d) The fluorescent images of CT26 cells co-stained with calcein-AM (green, live cells) and PI (red, dead cells) after various treatments. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic illustration of afterglow imaging of a living mouse. Fluorescence (b) and afterglow (c) imaging of mice after subcutaneous injection of CDs (10 mg mL−1, 25 μL). (d) Quantitative SNR values based on the images in b and c. (e) Afterglow imaging of tumors after injection of CDs (10 mg mL−1, 50 μL).

(a) Afterglow imaging of a CT26 tumor-bearing mouse after intratumoral injection of CDs (10 mg mL−1, 50 μL). The tumor site was preirradiated with laser (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2, 10 min), and then the images were collected after removing the laser. (b) Afterglow and fluorescence images of CDs solution (5 mg mL−1) covered with different thicknesses of chicken breast tissues. The CDs solution was pre-irradiated with laser (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2, 5 min) and then the images were collected after removing the laser.

(a) Animal therapeutic regimen. (b) Tumor volume, (c) Tumor weight, (d) The dynamic body weight graph, (e) Images of tumors, after various treatments for 14 d. (f) Routine blood analysis and (g) H&E staining of tumors upon treatment with PBS, PBS + L, CDs and CDs + L. (ns: not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001).
(a) Preparation of CDs; (b) Procedures of CDs for afterglow imaging and PDT.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for long-term near-infrared afterglow imaging and photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Imaging-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT) has the features of precise location and non-invasiveness. However, most of the photosensitizers (PSs) for PDT are poor fluorescent emitters, and the inadequate fluorescence is usually interfered by tissue auto-fluorescence. The afterglow phosphorescence could ideally address the issues of fluorescence imaging. Therefore, it is of double benefit to design phosphorescentCDs for enhanced near-infrared (NIR) phototheranostics. Hereon, we synthesized CDs with excellent biosafety, outstanding stability, good water dispersibility and effective photodynamic activity. CDs could emit NIR luminescence with long lifetime over 2 h, which afforded large tissue penetration (10 mm) and high SNR (98.4) for deep-tissue and long-term in vivo persistent luminescent imaging. Moreover, abundant reactive oxygen species generated by CDs enabled NIR afterglow imaging-guided PDT to effectively inhibit tumor growth. This work demonstrates the enormous potential of CDs as multifunctional nanoplatforms for biomedical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT), where photosensitizers (PSs) kill cancer cells by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) under the light irradiation [1–5], has attracted extensive attention due to its noninvasiveness, high regioselectivity and low drug resistance. Moreover, some PSs can be simultaneously utilized as probes to achieve optical imaging-guided PDT. Up to date, optical imaging modalities consisting mainly of fluorescence, bioluminescence and afterglow imaging, have a key role in real-time deciphering biological and physiological processes [6–9]. Nevertheless, fluorescence imaging relies on external real-time light excitation, which may result in strong tissue autofluorescence, thereby reducing the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) [10,11]. Although bioluminescence does not need the excitation of light and can solve the issue of autofluorescence, it depends on the specific reaction of enzyme and substrate to produce luminescence [12]. Afterglow or persistent luminescence imaging is activated before imaging and remain luminescent after the cessation of external illumination, which can significantly remove autofluorescence and enhance SNR [13–21]. In contrast to other imaging modes, afterglow imaging has the advantages of higher sensitivity and SNR, which has aroused increasing attention in theranostics.

Up to date, a variety of inorganic [22] and organic luminophores [23] have been developed for afterglow imaging. However, the clinical translation of phosphorsis still hindered by the disadvantages of long-term biotoxicity, instability, short emission wavelength and decay time. Therefore, it is of great concern to exploit near-infrared (NIR) phosphorescent PSs with excellent biosafety, outstanding stability, good water dispersibility, large penetration depth, long lifetime and high ROS generation ability.

As emerging luminescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have a wide range of applications in bioimaging [24–30] and cancer therapy [31–41] due to their superior optical performance, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [42–44]. Recently, some phosphorescent CDs with diverse emission wavelength and lifetime have come to the fore. Unfortunately, most of them have to be embedded into matrices [45] (e.g. silica [46] , covalent organic frameworks [47], zeolite [48] and boric acid [49,50]), and can only emit phosphorescence in the solid states[51]. Although afterglow can be obtained in some CDs aqueous solution [52–54], the wavelength of phosphorescence emission is relatively short. For all we know, scarcely any CDs have been simultaneously applied to afterglow imaging and PDT.

Herein, we started with the synthesis of solution-processable CDs via solvothermal method by using chlorin e6 (Ce6) and polyethylene imine (PEI) as starting materials (Scheme 1 ). On the one hand, the obtained CDs can serve as a phosphorescence initiator to emit intrinsic NIR luminescence at 680 nm with lifetime over 2.0 h, and the afterglow could be continuously reactivated with SNR of 63-fold higher than that of NIR fluorescence imaging. On the other hand, CDs also act as a ROS producer to achieve promoted photodynamic theranostics. For all we know, this is the first CDs-based NIR phosphor that renders afterglow imaging-guided PDT as effective cancer treatment.

CDs were prepared via solvothermal method by heating the mixture of Ce6 and PEI in N,N-dimethylformamide solution at 160 °C for 2 h (see supporting information). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 a) reveals the spherical morphology of CDs with the diameter of 10 ± 1.86 nm. There are three typical peaks centered at 400, 500 and 662 nm (Fig. 1b) in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs. The absorption peak at 400 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic C = C bond, and the absorption peaks at 500 and 662 nm are attributed to the n-π* transitions of C = O and C = N bonds. Under the excitation of 400, 500 or 662 nm, CDs can emit NIR light. However, the strongest emission at 672 nm is achieved under 400 nm excitation. Bright-field, fluorescence and afterglow photos of CDs were taken with the IVIS imaging system respectively. The brown aqueous solution of CDs could emit bright fluorescence and phosphorescence (Fig. 1c). The zeta-potential of CDs was + 1.77 mV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs (Figure S1) exhibits there are O-H/N-H (3273 cm−1), C-H (2927, 1431 and 1375 cm−1), C = O (1647 cm−1), C-N (1228 cm−1) and C-O (1130 cm−1) groups in CDs. Three characteristic peaks at 284.4, 399.2 and 530.7 eV in the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1d) prove that CDs consist of C, N and O, with the content of 43.25%, 25.06% and 31.69%, respectively. The three peaks (284.8, 287.2, and 287.9 eV) in the C 1 s high resolution (HR) XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) correspond to the C-C/C = C, C-N/C-O, and C = O groups, respectively. The two peaks at 399.1 and 401.5 eV in the HR N 1 s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1f) are attributed to C-N and N-H, respectively. The two peaks in the HR O 1 s spectrum at 530.8 and 532.5 eV (Figure S2) are ascribed to C-O and C = O, respectively.

Subsequently, we studied the influence of the concentration of CDs, laser (685 nm) irradiation time and laser power density on the afterglow luminescence of CDs. First, 1 mg mL−1 of CDs were irradiated by laser (0.8 W cm−2) for different time points (1, 3 and 5 min, Fig. 2 a). The afterglow intensity of CDs enhanced with the prolong of irradiation time. Then 1 mg mL−1 of CDs were irradiated by various power density of laser for 3 min, the phosphorescence grew brighter, as the power density rose from 0.1 to 0.8 W cm−2 (Fig. 2b). Finally, different concentrations of CDs were irradiated by 0.8 W cm−2 of laser for 3 min, the phosphorescence (Fig. 2c) became stronger, as the concentration was raised. The results demonstrated that the afterglow intensity of CDs is direct proportion to irradiation time, power density and concentration.

On the other hand, 2,7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) acted as an indicator to evaluate the 1O2 generation ability of CDs. After irradiation for 4 min, the fluorescence change of DCFH-DA alone was negligible. On the contrary, the fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA + CDs at 530 nm was significantly enhanced by 27.0-fold (Fig. 2d-f), indicating CDs could produce sufficient 1O2. Meanwhile, 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis (methylene) dimalonic acid (ABDA) was utilized as an indicator to detect the 1O2 generated by CDs. Upon the laser irradiation, the absorption of ABDA reducedsignificantly in the presence of CDs (Figure S3). On the contrary, the absorbance of ABDA did not changed (Figure S4). This result proves that CDs are able to generate singlet oxygen. Furthermore, the intracellular 1O2 generation capacity of CDs was also detected by DCFH-DA (Fig. 2g). No obvious fluorescence in the PBS, PBS + L and CDs groups was observed. However, intensegreen fluorescence appeared in the CDs + L group, demonstrating CDs possess high photodynamic activity.

The endocytosis of CDs was studied by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). CT26 was incubated with CDs (60 μg mL−1) at 37 °C. The red fluorescence at the cytoplasm verified that CDs could effectively enter the cells (Fig. 3 a). The red fluorescence signal in CT26 cells enhanced with the increase of incubation time, suggesting that CDs were internalized in a time-dependent manner. Then, CT26 cells incubated with CDs (1 mg mL−1) for 4 h were subjected to IVIS imaging system in the bioluminescence mode. It can be seen from Fig. 3b that CT26 cells could emit violent afterglow signal. We used 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay to study the in vitro dark toxicity and phototoxicity of CDs. Without irradiation, all of the cell viabilities were near 100%, indicating CDs had good cytocompatibility. By contrast, CDs could effectively inhibit the proliferation of CT26 cancer cells (Fig. 3c) under laser irradiation. When CT26 cells were treated with CDs (8 μg mL−1) and irradiated for 5 min, 85.0% of cells died, verifying the high photodynamic activity of CDs. Besides, the therapeutic efficacy of CDs was observed by calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) staining (Fig. 3d). For the PBS, PBS + L and CDs groups, CT26 cells emit green fluorescence, verifying that only light or CDs could not kill cancer cells. With the presence of light, CDs could cause severe photodynamic damage to cancer cells, and almost all cells were dead with intense red fluorescence.

The fluorescence and phosphorescence images were acquired on IVIS living imaging system in fluorescence and bioluminescence modes, respectively. The PBS solution of CDs (10 mg mL−1, 25 μL) was injected into the back of mice subcutaneously (Fig. 4 a). Fluorescence images were collected, and then CDs was activated by 685 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2) for 5 min to acquire afterglow images. Compared with the observed weak fluorescence signal (Fig. 4b), more intenseluminous signals could be detected (Fig. 4c). The quantification of imaging demonstrated that the SNR of fluorescence and afterglow was 1.6 and 98.4 (Fig. 4d), respectively.

Subsequently, CDs (10 mg mL−1, 50 μL) was intratumorally injected into CT26 tumor-bearing mice and then irradiated by laser (685 nm, 0.8 W cm−2) for 10 min. Strong phosphorescence signal was detected in the tumor site by the IVIS living imaging system. The afterglow signals could last for more than 120 min (Fig. 4e), which proves that CDs have a relatively long lifetime. In contrast, no afterglow signals could be acquired in the control groups (CDs, PBS, and PBS + L, Figure S5-S7), proving that only CDs plus laser irradiation can generate afterglow signals.

Inspiredby the above results, we further explored the sustainability of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, although the afterglow signals wore off after 2 h, it could be recharged by irradiation of laser for 10 min. After three cycles of renewal, the intensity of the afterglow imaging remained almost the same. Such high photostability and excellent recharging persistent phosphorescence was beneficial to achieve long-term in vivo imaging. The tissue penetration depth of afterglow luminescence of CDs was studied by placing different thicknesses of chicken breast tissues on the top of the pre-irradiated CDs solution (Fig. 5b). When the sample was covered with 2 mm chicken tissue, strong afterglow signal was detected. Nevertheless, the fluorescent signal was hardly detectable because of the short penetration depth of exciting light and high background interference of fluorescent signal. The continuous luminous signal could still be detected even under the cover of 10 mm chicken breast tissue, demonstrating improved imaging penetration depth of afterglow. Therefore, afterglow can eliminate the fluorescence noise of tissue itself, which is conducive to higher imaging depth and SNR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo anti-tumor therapy and biosafety
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the antitumor ability of CDs, a subcutaneous xenograft tumor model of CT26 was established in the armpit of Balb/c mice (Fig. 6 a). After implanting CT26 cells for 7 d, the tumor volume increased to about 80–100 mm3. Then the mice were randomly divided into four groups with different treatments, including PBS, PBS + L (0.3 W cm−2, 50 μL), CDs and CDs + L (0.3 W cm−2, 500 μg mL−1, 50 μL). In the PBS + L and CDs + L groups, the tumors were irradiated with laser (685 nm,10 min) after 1 h of intratumoral injection. During the treatment, the tumor size and mice weight were measured every other day. As shown in Fig. 6b, the tumor size in the PBS, PBS + L and CDs groups increased significantly, demonstrating that CDs or laser irradiation alone could not suppress tumor growth. By contrast, the tumor growth in the CDs + L group was drastically inhibited. After 15 d of treatment, the mice were sacrificed, the tumors and major organs were collected. For the CDs + L group, the tumor weight was lightest among the four groups (Fig. 6c), moreover, three of the tumors were completely ablated (Fig. 6e). The hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of tumor sections confirmed that CDs + L treatment induce the most serious cell necrosis (Fig. 6g). The results undoubtedly verified that CDs have high photodynamic antitumor efficacy.

We further assessed the biological safety of CDs by monitoring the weight changes of mice and routine blood tests. The body weight of mice present a trend of slow growth, proving CDs had ignorable side effect (Fig. 6d). Similar to the control groups, the parameters of routine blood test in the CDs + L group was within the normal range (Fig. 6f). Furthermore, there was no obvious histological damage in the major organs (Figure S8). These results manifested that the off-target toxicity of CDs was insignificant.

NIR luminescent CDs with favorable water solubility and benign biocompatibility were successfully synthesized. Benefiting from the merits of large penetration depth (10 mm) and long decay lifetime (over 2 h), CDs demonstrated supernal contrast and high sensitivity for luminescent afterglow imaging. After tumor localization, CDs could produce ROS to selectively ablate the tumors. In brief, we fabricate the first CDs-based photodynamic theranostic system that enables NIR afterglow imaging and PDT, and this scientific strategy will be further used to build up a new-fashioned kind of optical materials for effectual phototheranostics.
All animal experiments comply with the ARRIVE guidelines and the British Animal (Scientific Procedures) Act 1986 and related guidelines.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.143384.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
